title: Cut-flush boards, trimming the covers level with the text block
subtitle: How to produce a paperback-style book with boards trimmed after casing-in
excerpt: In a cut-flush binding the covers are trimmed to the same size as the pages after the book is cased in. There is no overhang at the foredge, head, or tail. The result is a neat, modernist appearance suited to notebooks and pocket reference books.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on board attachment and trimming: discusses variations in board projection and the flush-cut style as a utilitarian finish common in trade bindings. Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), notes on the flush-cover style in commercial binding from the 1880s onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: The amount by which the board projects beyond the edges of the text block, typically 3-5 mm. Cut-flush bindings have zero square.
The binding cover made separately from the text block, then glued to it. A case consists of two boards and a spine piece covered in cloth or paper.

content.text: Standard hard case-bound books have a
, the boards project a few millimetres beyond the text block on three sides. The square protects the page edges. A cut-flush binding removes this projection: the

and the text block are trimmed together after the book is assembled, so all edges are level. The technique originated in trade bookbinding as a means of producing an even stack of volumes. At home scale it suits notebooks and pocket books where compactness matters.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: Trimming through assembled boards requires multiple clean passes of a sharp blade, not a single heavy cut. Use a fresh blade. Keep the ruler held firmly, the pressure of cutting through card causes the ruler to walk if not anchored.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm
items.notes: Cut oversize, 10 mm larger on all three trimming edges (foredge, head, tail). The excess is trimmed flush after casing-in.

items.name: Craft knife with fresh blades
items.notes: Multiple passes through assembled boards blunts blades quickly.

items.name: Metal ruler, 60 cm
items.notes: Long rule for trim cuts across the full height of the book.

items.name: Cutting mat, A3
items.notes: Large enough to support the whole book.

content.text: Make the case with boards cut 10 mm oversize at foredge, head, and tail. Attach the boards at the spine in the normal way and allow to dry fully under weight, at least 24 hours. Do not trim before the adhesive is fully dry; a damp board distorts under the knife.

Measure the intended final dimensions from the spine and the head of the text block. Mark the foredge, head, and tail trim lines on the front board with pencil. Check that the lines are square to the spine before cutting.
Trim front board, text block, and back board

Open the book to the first page. Trim the foredge of the front board first: align the metal rule on the pencil line and make four or five clean passes through the board with the craft knife, increasing pressure on each pass. Continue through the text block pages, paper cuts more easily than board, so reduce pressure mid-stack. Close the book and trim the back board to match the front, using the cut front board as a measurement guide. Trim the head and tail in the same sequence: front board, text block, back board.

Lightly sand the trimmed edges with 240-grit sandpaper on a flat block to smooth any tearout from the blade. A beeswax block rubbed along the foredge seals the paper fibres and gives the edge a slight sheen, the traditional finish on well-made trade bindings.
